title: Everything Is Expensive. Treat Yourself to One of These Amazon Prime Day Picks Under $30
gist: WIRED's Prime Day under-$30 deals guide (June 24, 2200 words, Molly Higgins), framed explicitly with 'Thirty dollars doesn't get you as much as it used to' and 'as the economy worsens, Buy Now Pay Later services boom, daily bills skyrocket, and inflation continues to surge.' Products span home/beauty, tech accessories, and kitchen items—notably non-tech categories like Laneige lip mask ($16), Bagsmart packing cubes ($24), Furbo pet camera ($27), Loop Quiet 2 earplugs ($20), iPhone cases from Spigen and Beats, and the Liquid I.V. hydration multiplier ($16).

themes.theme: Consumer Economic Distress as Explicit Editorial Frame

insights.claim: WIRED's Prime Day under-$30 deals guide opened with the explicit statement that 'Thirty dollars doesn't get you as much as it used to. In times like these, as the economy worsens, Buy Now, Pay Later services boom, daily bills skyrocket, and inflation continues to surge'—direct editorial acknowledgment of consumer financial stress in a tech publication.
insights.speaker: Molly Higgins
insights.so_what: WIRED explicitly citing BNPL boom, bill increases, and inflation in a consumer tech deals guide is a strong qualitative signal that tech media now sees its audience as economically stressed—a departure from historically premium-skewing tech editorial voice that may foreshadow softness in discretionary consumer electronics.
insights.quote: Thirty dollars doesn't get you as much as it used to. In times like these, as the economy worsens, "Buy Now, Pay Later" services boom, daily bills skyrocket, and inflation continues to surge, it's more important than ever to make that dollar stretch.

themes.theme: Non-Tech Categories Infiltrating Tech Media Deals Coverage

insights.claim: WIRED's under-$30 Prime Day guide included Laneige Lip Sleeping Mask ($16, 33% off), Bagsmart Compression Packing Cubes ($24, 44% off), Liquid I.V. Hydration Multiplier ($16, 30% off), and Celsius energy drink variety pack ($17, 15% off)—non-tech consumer goods comprising roughly half the article's recommendations.
insights.speaker: Molly Higgins
insights.so_what: WIRED devoting roughly half of a Prime Day deals guide to beauty, food, and wellness products signals that Amazon's Prime Day is capturing mindshare as a general consumer shopping event rather than a tech-specific event—expanding Amazon's competitive footprint against Walmart and Target on non-tech retail.
insights.quote: We've been scouring to find the best discounts and deals on WIRED-tested and -approved gear for less than that for Prime Day.

insights.claim: Furbo Mini 360 pet camera was listed at $27 (45% off from $49) as 'the best pet camera you can get for under $30,' with WIRED noting its optional 'Furbo Nanny' subscription service that provides AI-categorized daily highlights, safety alerts, and pet behavior detection.
insights.speaker: Molly Higgins
insights.so_what: Furbo embedding a subscription 'Nanny' service into a $27 hardware device mirrors the razor-and-blades SaaS model in consumer pet tech—a signal of how pet camera makers are pursuing recurring revenue from budget hardware sales.
insights.quote: In my opinion, this is the best pet camera you can get for under $30.
